WaterCheck every 2-3 days.
Top up with plain water only between nutrient feeds.
Plant FoodWeeks 1-2: water only.
Week 3: half dose.
Week 4+: full dose weekly.

Target EC:
greens 1.0-1.6 · herbs 1.4-2.0 · fruiting 2.0-3.0.

Top up with plain water between feeds.
Gardyn pods: follow their dosing exactly.
pH TargetAim for 5.5-6.5.
Tap water is often pH 7-8.
Use pH Down or white vinegar to reach 6.0.
Full DrainEvery 2-3 weeks.
Add a few ml of 3% H₂O₂ to fresh water on refill.
OverfeedBrown tips or white crust:
flush plain water for one week,
then restart at half dose.
Lighting16 hrs on / 8 hrs off.
Don't reduce below 14 hrs or growth stalls.
Never break the dark cycle.

White column = today · orange = incomplete today · sandy red = missed (a past due day) · grey = upcoming · green = done · blank = nothing due. Tap today’s orange squares to complete them, and they turn green. Saved with your Thryve layout.

·
Scheduled Incomplete Missed Done
.ics works in Apple Calendar / Reminders & Outlook; Google needs Settings → Import (or the Google button does a quick-add).

Today’s task cards

Grow Log

Ask your grow assistant, or read its automatic check-ins synced from the cloud.

Sprouting & New Pod Setup
Gardyn Soak Method · Sprout Nursery Tray · DIY Nursery
Rockwool Prep · The 5-Minute Soak
Use the Gardyn Sprout Nursery tray · plain fresh water only, no pH adjustment
  • Insert tray into nursery base · add plain water up to the indent between tray squares
  • Place yCubes in tray and soak for exactly 5 minutes
  • Lift tray and drain all water from the base completely
  • Return tray into empty base and place the lid on top
  • Goal: moist but never sitting in standing water · oversaturation slows germination
Each new pod
Seeding & Germination
Nursery goes on top of the Gardyn lid · not inside the tower yet
  • Sow several seeds per cube, ¼ inch deep · Gardyn cubes are sown densely, not one-per-cube
  • Leafy greens & herbs (lettuce, arugula, cilantro, chives): sow 6–10 and grow as a bunch · just prune, don't thin to one
  • Fruiting & large plants (tomato, pepper, cucumber, kale): sow 2–3, then thin to the strongest seedling
  • Place covered nursery on top of Gardyn lid to benefit from lights and ambient warmth
  • Check moisture weekly · dark green & moist = fine · light green & dry = re-soak 5 min
  • Lettuce & leafy greens: 3–7 days · Herbs: 5–14 days · Flowers: 5–21 days
  • Never add plant food during germination · plain water only the entire time
Each new pod
When to Move to the Tower
Wait for one of these three signs before transferring out of the nursery
  • True leaves are visible (not just the first seed leaves)
  • Roots are 0.5 inches long emerging from the yCube
  • Cotyledons (first seed leaves) are yellowing · seed's stored energy is running out
Per seedling
Pod Placement
Light intensity increases toward the top · position plants accordingly
  • Lettuce → lower pods (bolts faster under intense LED heat)
  • Kale & spinach → mid to upper pods
  • Flowers → lower or mid pods so they don't shade food plants
  • Leave one empty pod between large leafy greens to prevent shading
At planting
Pod Placement in Tower
Light intensity increases toward the top · position plants accordingly
  • Lettuce → lower pods (bolts faster under intense LED heat)
  • Kale & spinach → mid to upper pods
  • Flowers → lower or mid pods so they don't shade food plants
  • Leave one empty pod between large leafy greens to prevent shading
At transfer
Germination Timeline: Day 1 soak 5 min, plant, place nursery on Gardyn lid · Days 3–7 leafy greens sprout · Days 5–14 herbs sprout · Days 5–21 flowers sprout · Once true leaves or 0.5" roots appear → transfer to tower · Week 3 in tower start feeding · Weeks 4–5 first harvest (lettuce/spinach) · Weeks 6–8 kale ready
DIY yCubes, Pods & Nursery
Store-bought rockwool · DIY trays · 3D printed pods · reused pods
Prepping Store-Bought Rockwool (Grodan / Cultilene)
Rockwool is alkaline from the factory · skip the pH prep and germination rates drop significantly
  • Use 2-inch cubes (Grodan Hugo or Delta are standard fits for Gardyn pod sleeves)
  • Mix a bucket of water to pH 5.5 using pH Down or a small splash of white vinegar
  • Submerge cubes fully and soak for 30–60 minutes · this neutralizes the alkaline pH
  • Drain but do not squeeze or wring · rockwool retains the right moisture level on its own
  • Plant seeds immediately: push 2–3 seeds ¼ inch into the center dimple of each cube
  • Place seeded cubes into Gardyn pod sleeves or 3D-printed pod shells before going into the tower
Per batch
Fixing a Tilted Plant That's Transplanted
When a transplanted seedling leans from an uneven cube or top-heavy growth
  • Wedge it upright: gently pack a small piece of damp sponge or foam alongside the rockwool cube inside the pod
  • Reposition the pod: some Gardyn pod openings allow a slight angle adjustment
  • Rebalance: trim heavy growth on the leaning side to even out the plant's weight
As needed
DIY Trellis & Staking
A simple support for tall or floppy plants · cheap and reusable
  • Push a bamboo skewer, chopstick, or thin dowel straight down into the pod alongside the stem, into the rockwool
  • Tie the stem to it loosely with a twist tie, soft plant tape, or a strip of cloth · leave room to grow, never cinch it tight
  • For taller plants use a longer dowel, or lean two skewers into a small teepee and tie across them
  • Check the ties every week or two and loosen them as the stem thickens so they don't cut in
As needed
DIY Sprouting Tray
No need to buy the official Gardyn Sprout Nursery · a disposable aluminum baking pan or a clear-lidded takeout container makes an excellent germination tray.
  • A half-size foil baking pan or any clear takeout container with a lid both work well
  • Add only a shallow layer of pH 5.5 water · just enough to keep cubes damp, never submerged
  • The clear lid (or loosely tented plastic wrap) acts as a humidity dome to speed germination
  • Set the tray on top of the Gardyn lid so LED warmth from below keeps cubes cozy
  • Vent or remove the lid once sprouts appear to prevent damping-off
Per batch
Running the Nursery
However you build your tray, the method is the same · keep it humid and warm until the seedlings are ready for the tower
  • Use any shallow container with a lid or plastic wrap · maintains humidity for germination
  • Place pH-prepped rockwool cubes inside, add a small amount of pH 5.5 water to the bottom (just enough to keep cubes moist, not submerged)
  • Place on top of the Gardyn · the LED warmth from below improves germination rates
  • Check moisture every 2 days: cubes should feel damp, never dry, never sitting in pooled water
  • Remove lid once seedlings emerge to prevent damping off (fungal rot from humidity)
  • Transfer to tower once true leaves appear or roots reach 0.5 inches
Per batch
Using 3D Printed or Third-Party Pod Shells
Community-designed pod shells are available on Printables and Thingiverse · standard Gardyn pod dimensions are 1.5 inches diameter
  • Ensure the pod outer diameter matches the Gardyn column slot · 1.5" is the standard
  • Rinse new 3D-printed pods in pH-neutral water before use to remove any print residue
  • Insert pH-prepped 2-inch rockwool cube snugly · it should be held in place but not crushed
  • Works identically to official yCubes once seeded and transferred to the tower
Per pod
Reusing yCubes & Seed Pod Shells
The plastic pod shell is reusable for years · the rockwool cube inside is single-use and gets swapped out each crop
  • Pop the spent plant and its rockwool cube out of the shell · compost or bin the cube, old roots and all (don't replant in used rockwool · it holds stale salts and root pathogens)
  • Pull or rinse any leftover root threads out of the shell so nothing clogs or rots against the next cube
  • Soak the empty shell in a 1:3 white vinegar to water solution for 15 minutes to kill mold and bacteria, then rinse thoroughly and air-dry
  • Check the shell for cracks or warping before reuse · a split shell won't hold the cube centered in the column slot
  • Drop in a fresh pH-prepped 2-inch cube, seed as normal, and return the shell to the nursery or tower
Per crop
💡 DIY Note: Reused pod shells should be soaked in a 1:3 white vinegar to water solution for 15 minutes, then rinsed thoroughly and air-dried before reuse. Vinegar is a food-safe way to kill mold and bacteria between crops without the residue or fumes of bleach.
Plant Food & Hydro Boost
Feeding schedule · reservoir management · pH targets
Feeding Schedule
Seedlings are sensitive · introduce nutrients gradually
  • Weeks 1–2: plain pH-adjusted water only, zero nutrients
  • Week 3: start at half the recommended dose
  • Week 4 onward: full dose added weekly at reservoir top-up
  • After full drain: always mix fresh nutrients into clean water before replacing
Weekly (week 3+)
Nutrient Mix for Leafy Greens
Nitrogen-forward formulas · lighter EC than fruiting plants
  • Target EC: 1.0–1.6 for lettuce, spinach, kale
  • Target pH in reservoir: 5.5–6.5 · check weekly
  • Good options: General Hydroponics Flora Series, Masterblend 4-18-38
  • Overfeeding signs: brown crispy leaf edges, white salt crust on rockwool → flush with plain water for 1 week
Weekly
Reservoir Maintenance
Clean water is the foundation of healthy roots and strong growth
  • Top up with plain water between weekly nutrient additions · plants drink more water than nutrients
  • Full drain + clean every 2–3 weeks · prevents salt buildup and algae
  • Keep reservoir lid tight · light entering causes algae blooms
  • Keep water temperature below 72°F · warm water holds less oxygen and accelerates root rot
  • Add a few ml of 3% H₂O₂ to clean water on refill to kill root rot bacteria
Every 2–3 weeks
pH Management & Hydroboost
Why pH matters · what to use · when to feed
About pH · Should You Adjust?
Gardyn says tap water is fine · and it often is. But optimizing pH is the single biggest yield improvement you can make.
  • Plants absorb nutrients best between pH 5.5–6.5. Outside this range, nutrients are present but locked out · the plant can't use them even if you're feeding correctly.
  • Most tap water runs pH 7.5–8.0. At this level, iron and manganese become unavailable, causing yellowing leaves even with good nutrition.
  • Gardyn's tap water guidance works for casual growing. For serious yield and health, pH adjustment makes a noticeable difference within 2–3 weeks.
  • You don't need to adjust every fill · just check weekly and correct when outside the 5.5–6.5 range.
Weekly check
Buy a pH Pen Recommended
The single best investment for your Gardyn after the machine itself
  • Best value: Apera PH20 (~$35) · accurate, reliable, widely used by hydro growers
  • Also excellent: BlueLab pH Pen (~$50) · industry standard, very durable
  • Budget option: API Freshwater Master Test Kit (~$10 at pet stores) · liquid drop test, less precise but works
  • Store pH pen tip submerged in storage solution (or KCl solution) · never let it dry out
  • Calibrate monthly with pH 7.0 calibration solution for accurate readings
One-time purchase
How to Adjust pH
Simple once you have a pen · takes 2 minutes per reservoir fill
  • Lower pH: add a few drops of white vinegar or pH Down solution, stir, re-test
  • Raise pH: add a few drops of baking soda solution or pH Up solution
  • Add slowly · pH swings are worse than being slightly off. Aim for 6.0 as your target.
  • After adding nutrients, always re-check pH · nutrients shift it
Each reservoir fill
Hydroboost · Nutrient Feeding Guide
What to use, when to start, and how much
  • Gardyn brand: Follow their pod dosing exactly · designed for the system
  • General Hydroponics Flora Series: 3-part system (Grow, Micro, Bloom) · most flexible, excellent results. Use the "Vegetative" schedule for herbs and greens.
  • Masterblend 4-18-38: Popular DIY option, very cost-effective, mix with calcium nitrate and Epsom salt per standard ratios
  • Start at half dose in week 3, full dose from week 4 onward
  • Target EC 1.0–1.6 for leafy greens, 1.4–2.0 for herbs, 2.0–3.0 for fruiting plants
  • Signs of overfeeding: brown crispy leaf tips, white salt crust on rockwool → flush for 1 week
Weekly (week 3+)
Nutrient Amounts
Starting points for a ~2.5 gal Gardyn tank · always confirm with an EC meter and follow the label
  • Gardyn Plant Food: follow the bottle · it's pre-measured for the 2.5 gal tank (a capful at refill on their schedule). Don't substitute a guessed amount.
  • GH Flora Trio (greens): per full tank 2½ tsp Micro + 2½ tsp Gro + 1¼ tsp Bloom (1 + 1 + ½ tsp per gallon) · lands near EC 1.0 to 1.4.
  • GH MaxiGro (1-part): 2½ tsp per full tank (1 tsp per gallon) · simplest option, near EC 1.2 to 1.5.
  • Masterblend 4-18-38 (DIY): per full tank 1¼ tsp Masterblend + 1¼ tsp Calcium Nitrate + ⅔ tsp Epsom salt (½ + ½ + ¼ tsp per gallon, or weigh 2.4 + 2.4 + 1.2 g per gallon for accuracy).
  • Spoon guide: 3 tsp = 1 tbsp. These are small amounts, so a teaspoon is most accurate · reach for a tablespoon only when pre-mixing several gallons at once.
  • Target EC by crop: greens 1.0 to 1.6 · herbs 1.4 to 2.0 · fruiting 2.0 to 3.0.
Reference
Mixing Your Nutrients Properly
The order and method matter more than the exact amount · do this and you avoid most feeding problems
  • Never combine concentrates directly. Pouring undiluted parts together makes them react and lock out (you'll see cloudiness or sludge). Always add each one into the water separately.
  • Multi-part (GH Flora): add Micro to the water first and stir fully, then Gro and stir, then Bloom. The Micro-first order prevents nutrient lockout.
  • Masterblend: dissolve Masterblend + Epsom in part of the water, dissolve Calcium Nitrate separately in more water, then combine into the full volume · keeping calcium apart until diluted stops it clouding.
  • Stir until fully dissolved · undissolved powder won't feed the plants and can clog the pump.
  • Use room-temperature water · very cold water dissolves powders poorly.
  • Adjust pH last, after everything is mixed, since nutrients shift it · target 5.5 to 6.5.
  • Half strength the first feeding week, full dose from week 4 · top up between feeds with plain water.
Every mix
💡 Bottom line on pH: Gardyn's tap water advice is perfectly fine for casual growing. If you want maximum yields and the healthiest plants possible, a $35 pH pen and a bottle of pH Down will pay for themselves within your first harvest. It's optional, not mandatory.
Nutrient Burn Guide
Identifying, treating & preventing burned plants
How to Identify Nutrient Burn
Burn starts at the leaf tips and edges · it always moves inward from the outside
  • Stage 1 · Tip burn: leaf tips turn yellow, then brown and crispy. Very common and easy to catch early.
  • Stage 2 · Edge burn: browning spreads along the whole leaf margin, leaves curl slightly
  • Stage 3 · Full leaf damage: large brown patches, leaves become papery and brittle · hard to recover from
  • White crusty deposits on rockwool = salt buildup from overfeeding, a sure sign of burn
  • Don't confuse with mildew · burn is brown/crispy, mildew is white/powdery
Check weekly
Treating Active Nutrient Burn
Act at stage 1 or 2 · stage 3 leaves won't recover but the plant can still be saved
  • Step 1: Drain the reservoir completely
  • Step 2: Refill with plain pH-adjusted water only · zero nutrients
  • Step 3: Run plain water for a full week to flush salt buildup from the rockwool and roots
  • Step 4: Remove all visibly burned leaves · they won't recover and drain energy from the plant
  • Step 5: After 1 week, reintroduce nutrients at half dose and build back up gradually
As needed
Light Burn vs Nutrient Burn
Both look similar but have different causes and fixes
  • Nutrient burn: starts at tips and edges of all leaves across the plant
  • Light burn: affects only the leaves closest to the LEDs · tops of plants touching or near the lights
  • Fix for light burn: prune the plant back so no leaves are within 2–3 inches of the LED bar
  • Fix for nutrient burn: flush reservoir as above
As needed
Prevention
Burn is almost entirely preventable with consistent habits
  • Always start new plants at half dose · never go straight to full strength
  • Top up reservoir with plain water between nutrient additions · don't add nutrients every top-up
  • Do a full drain + clean every 2–3 weeks to reset salt accumulation
  • Leafy greens are more sensitive than herbs · keep EC at 1.0–1.6, not higher
  • If in doubt, less nutrients is always safer than more
Ongoing
Pruning & Harvesting
Cut-and-come-again technique · plant-by-plant guide
Leafy Greens · Outer Leaf Method
Always from the outside in · the center crown is sacred
  • Cut at the base of each leaf stem with sharp scissors · never pull or tear
  • Never remove more than ⅓ of the plant in one session
  • Leave the center growing point completely untouched
  • Harvest before leaves touch the LEDs or neighboring pods · contact scorches leaves
Weekly
Basil · Node Pruning
Pinching above nodes triggers bushier lateral growth and delays bolting
  • Cut stem just above a pair of leaves (the node) · never mid-stem
  • Pinch flower buds the moment they appear · do not let them open
  • Bolted basil: cut back 50%, leaving 2–3 nodes above the base
  • Bolted basil leaves are still edible · more bitter, best cooked or in pesto
Weekly
Rosemary & Woody Herbs
Light, frequent trimming keeps shape and pushes new growth
  • Snip 1–2 inches from stem tips only · never cut into old woody growth
  • Harvest sprigs regularly; neglect causes legginess
Every 2 weeks
Mint
Aggressive grower · cut it hard, it always comes back
  • Cut back to 3–4 inches tall when overgrown · it will bush back within days
  • Trim runners that escape into neighboring pods regularly
As needed
Flowers · Deadheading
Removing spent blooms forces the plant to produce new ones
  • Pinch or snip spent flowers at the base of the bloom stem as soon as petals fade
  • Remove fallen petals from the reservoir area immediately · they cause rot
  • Best flowers for your Gardyn: nasturtiums, violas, marigolds, chamomile, borage
Weekly
Powdery Mildew Prevention
Treatment rotation · environmental controls
Active Outbreak Protocol In Order
Follow these steps in sequence · don't skip to sprays before removing affected leaves
  • Step 1: Remove any leaf more than 30% white · bag and trash immediately
  • Step 2 (Day 1): H₂O₂ spray · 1 part 3% H₂O₂ to 3 parts water, both sides of all leaves
  • Step 3 (Day 3): Baking soda spray · 1 tsp baking soda + 1 tsp dish soap + 1 quart water
  • Step 4 (Day 5): Neem oil spray · few drops neem + 1 tsp dish soap + 1 quart water
  • Step 5: Rotate all three weekly until fully clear (2–3 weeks typically)
As needed
Weekly Prevention Sprays
Alternate to prevent resistance building · apply in the morning only
  • Week A: Baking soda spray (1 tsp baking soda + 1 tsp dish soap + 1 qt water)
  • Week B: Neem oil spray (few drops neem + 1 tsp dish soap + 1 qt water, shake constantly)
  • Always apply in the morning · leaves must dry fully before lights cycle off
Weekly
Environmental Controls
Airflow is more effective than any spray · fix the environment first
  • Place a small USB fan near the towers · even gentle airflow stops spore settlement
  • Keep room temperature 65–80°F and humidity 50–70%
  • Keep leaves from touching each other or the tower walls
  • Thin dense canopies regularly · mildew thrives in stagnant air pockets
Ongoing
Pest Identification & Treatment
Know what you're dealing with before you treat
Fungus Gnats Most Common
Tiny flies hovering near the base · larvae live in wet growing medium and damage roots
  • Yellow sticky traps placed near base catch adults immediately
  • Let rockwool dry slightly between watering cycles · larvae need moisture
  • Apply diluted H₂O₂ (1:4 ratio) directly to rockwool to kill larvae
  • Neem oil drench in the reservoir as ongoing prevention
As needed
Spider Mites
Tiny dots on leaves, fine webbing underneath · thrive in hot dry conditions
  • Check leaf undersides with a magnifying glass · look for tiny moving dots
  • Wipe leaves with damp cloth first to remove visible mites physically
  • Spray neem oil solution every 3 days for 2 weeks to break the egg cycle
  • Increase humidity slightly · mites hate moisture
As needed
Aphids
Soft-bodied clusters on new growth and stem tips · reproduce fast
  • Blast off with a firm stream of water first · effective for small infestations
  • Follow with neem oil spray on all affected areas
  • Check daily until gone · aphid populations double every few days if untreated
As needed
Root Care & Tower Upkeep
Below the surface · most overlooked part of hydro maintenance
Root Inspection
Healthy roots are white or cream-colored and slightly fuzzy
  • Gently pull each pod slightly to check roots below
  • White & fuzzy: perfect · leave alone
  • Light tan/beige: normal nutrient staining · fine
  • Brown & slimy: root rot · treat immediately
  • Dark brown & mushy: too far gone · remove the pod
Every 2 weeks
Root Trimming Monthly
Roots reaching the pump clog it and starve other plants of water flow
  • Do during reservoir drain so roots are fully visible
  • Sterilize scissors with rubbing alcohol before starting
  • Trim only roots clogging the reservoir or wrapping the pump · not roots inside the column
  • Never remove more than ⅓ of root mass at once
  • Rinse trimmed roots under clean water before returning pod
Monthly
Root Rot Treatment Act Fast
Spreads to neighboring pods quickly · don't wait
  • Remove affected pod from tower immediately
  • Rinse roots gently under cool running water
  • Trim all brown/mushy material with sterilized scissors
  • Soak remaining roots in 1 part 3% H₂O₂ to 5 parts water for 10 minutes
  • Do full reservoir drain + clean before returning pod
  • Add weekly H₂O₂ dose to reservoir to prevent recurrence
As needed
Pump & Water Flow Check
A failing pump can kill every plant in under 24 hours · check regularly
  • Listen for the pump · should run quietly and consistently
  • Confirm water is flowing from the top and trickling through all pods
  • Weak flow: check for root clog first, then inspect pump inlet
  • Remove pump monthly, rinse under warm water, clear intake debris
Monthly
Tower Column Cleaning
Algae and mineral buildup inside columns restrict water flow over time
  • Remove all pods, rinse column interior with warm water
  • Bottle brush for green algae growth inside columns
  • White crusty deposits: soak with 1:1 white vinegar + water for 15 min, then scrub
  • Rinse thoroughly · vinegar residue affects pH
Every 4–6 weeks
Pod & Rockwool Hygiene
Old rockwool harbors bacteria · replace between every crop
  • Always remove rockwool when pulling a finished plant · never leave it in
  • Soak empty pods in H₂O₂ or white vinegar solution for 30 min
  • Scrub root debris from inside pod, let air dry fully before new rockwool
  • Never reuse rockwool from a plant that had root rot or mildew
Each pod turnover
Between-Cycle Deep Clean
A full reset when a tower's worth of plants finishes · prevents salt buildup and disease carryover
  • Pull all pods, remove and discard the old rockwool, and unplug the pump
  • Empty the reservoir completely · wipe out any sludge and root debris
  • Descale: fill the tank with 1 part white vinegar to 3 parts water (about 2 to 3 cups of vinegar in the ~2.5 gal tank) and run the pump 30 to 60 min to flush the lines and column
  • Column: scrub the inside with a bottle brush · for stubborn white mineral crust, soak it with 1:1 vinegar and water for 15 min first
  • Pods: soak in 1:3 vinegar and water (or H₂O₂) for 30 min, scrub out root debris, rinse, and air dry fully
  • Sanitize, optional: after the vinegar rinse, wipe surfaces with 1 part 3% H₂O₂ to 3 parts water to kill lingering bacteria or mildew
  • Rinse everything thoroughly with clean water · leftover vinegar or peroxide will swing your pH
  • Refill with fresh pH-adjusted water, add nutrients, and start the next crop with freshly pH-prepped cubes
Each crop cycle
Golden rule: Good water flow + clean reservoir + no light leaks into the reservoir = healthy roots almost guaranteed. Most root problems trace back to one of these three.
The Full Maintenance Table
Everything at a glance
Task Notes Frequency
Check reservoir water levelTop up with plain water if lowEvery 2–3 days
Harvest outer leavesNever more than ⅓ of plantWeekly
Add plant foodFrom week 3 onward; half dose first weekWeekly
Check reservoir pHTarget 5.5–6.5Weekly
Mildew prevention sprayAlternate baking soda / neem · morning onlyWeekly
Inspect for pests & mildewCheck leaf undersides carefullyWeekly
Prune & shape herbsPinch basil buds before they openWeekly
Deadhead spent flowersRemove fallen petals from reservoir area tooWeekly
Full reservoir drain + cleanAdd H₂O₂ to clean water on refillEvery 2–3 weeks
Root inspectionWhite & fuzzy = healthy · Brown & slimy = treatEvery 2 weeks
Triage declining podsRemove past-saving plants, prep new rockwoolEvery 2–3 weeks
Root trimmingDuring drain; sterilize scissors firstMonthly
Pump check & cleanClear root debris from intakeMonthly
Tower column scrubBottle brush + vinegar for mineral depositsEvery 4–6 weeks
Pod cleaning on turnoverH₂O₂ soak + scrub + air dryEach pod change
Check water temperatureKeep below 72°F to prevent root rotWeekly in summer
Large Plants & Transplanting
Staking · moving to soil · large plants
Transplanting to Soil · Step by Step
For plants that have outgrown the Gardyn · hibiscus, fruiting plants, large herbs
  • Prep a pot (12–14 inches minimum for hibiscus) ⅓ filled with well-draining potting mix
  • Remove pod slowly · unwind any roots tangled in the column, never yank
  • Leave the rockwool cube intact around the roots · it breaks down in soil naturally
  • Set plant in pot center, rockwool about 1 inch below the rim, fill and press gently
  • Water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom
  • Critical: keep in indirect light for 1 week · never straight into full sun from indoor LEDs
  • Gradually increase sun exposure over the following week
  • Expect some drooping for 2–3 days · normal transplant shock, don't overwater
Per plant
Flowers that actually thrive in the Gardyn: Nasturtiums, violas, marigolds, chamomile, borage · all compact, edible, and happy under indoor LEDs. Petunias work too with consistent deadheading. Avoid anything that gets large or needs a dormancy period.
Recommended Accessories
Tools worth having for a healthy Gardyn

pH Pen / Digital pH Meter

The single most impactful tool you can add to your Gardyn routine. Tap water typically runs pH 7–8, which is too alkaline for hydroponic growing · nutrients become unavailable to roots at the wrong pH, even when the water looks perfect. A reliable pH pen lets you hit the 5.5–6.5 sweet spot every time. Look for one with automatic temperature compensation (ATC). Calibrate monthly with pH 4.0 and 7.0 calibration solution.

Recommended: Apera Instruments PH20 or BlueLab pH Pen · both are accurate, durable, and calibrate easily.

🔗 Amazon link · coming soon

Rockwool / Stonewool Grow Cubes

The growing medium inside every Gardyn yCube is rockwool · spun basalt rock that holds moisture and air simultaneously, which is why roots love it. When DIY-ing pods, you need the right cube size to fit Gardyn pod slots: 2-inch cubes (Grodan Hugo or similar) are the standard fit.

Key prep note: rockwool is alkaline out of the bag (pH ~7.5–8.0). Always pre-soak in pH 5.5 water for 30–60 minutes before use or seeds will struggle to germinate.

🔗 Amazon link · coming soon

Plant Nutrients · GH Flora Series or Masterblend 4-18-38

General Hydroponics Flora Series is a 3-part liquid system (Grow, Micro, Bloom) · the most flexible option and excellent for mixed Gardyn pods. Use the Vegetative schedule for herbs and greens. Widely available.

Masterblend 4-18-38 is a dry powder alternative · very cost-effective and popular in the DIY hydro community. Mix with calcium nitrate and Epsom salt per standard ratios (12g Masterblend + 12g calcium nitrate + 6g Epsom salt per 5 gallons).

Either way: weeks 1-2 plain water only, week 3 half dose, week 4 onward full dose. Never add nutrients to a dry reservoir · mix into water first.

🔗 Amazon link · coming soon
💡 More accessories to add: Small USB fan (powdery mildew prevention), EC/TDS meter (nutrient strength), 3% hydrogen peroxide (root care + mildew), bamboo skewers (staking tall plants).
Light placement guide. In the Gardyn, light intensity increases the higher up the pod. Use this to decide where to place each plant for best results.
High Light · Upper Pods (1–10)
Full LED intensity · 16 hrs on / 8 hrs off · strongest growers, herbs & fruiting plants go here
Banana Peppers
Upper pods · warm season · needs full intensity to fruit
FruitingEdible
Basil
Upper pods · loves intensity · bolts faster if too low
HerbEdible
Bi-color Sunflower
Upper pods · blooms best under bright light
Flower
Blush Hibiscus
Upper pods · blooms best under bright light
Flower
Bunching Onions
Upper pods · similar to chives · harvest from base upward
HerbEdible
Burrito Pepper
Upper pods · warm season · needs full intensity to fruit
FruitingEdible
Candy Cane Pepper
Upper pods · warm season · needs full intensity to fruit
FruitingEdible
Canna Flower
Upper pods · blooms best under bright light
Flower
Celery
Upper pods · slow grower · needs strong light for best crunch
Leafy GreenEdible
Celosia Mix
Upper pods · blooms best under bright light
Flower
Cherry Tomatoes
Upper pods · needs maximum light · stake as it grows
FruitingEdible
Chives
Any position · thrives at high intensity · cut-and-regrow repeatedly
HerbEdible

Light, Climate & Environment
The invisible factors that control everything
Light Cycle
Consistent light cycles prevent stress and premature bolting
  • Herbs & leafy greens: 16 hrs on / 8 hrs off
  • Flowers: 12 hrs on / 12 hrs off encourages more blooming
  • Never change the cycle suddenly · shift by 30 min per day if adjusting
  • Use the Gardyn app timer if available · inconsistency causes stress bolting
Set & leave
Temperature & Humidity
Outside these ranges is when mildew, root rot, and bolting spike
  • Ideal temperature: 65–80°F (18–27°C)
  • Ideal humidity: 50–70% · above 70% is mildew territory
  • Reservoir water: keep below 72°F · warm water holds less oxygen → root rot
  • A small USB fan near the garden helps with both airflow and humidity
Monitor weekly
pH Management Without a Pen
pH 5.5–6.5 is the sweet spot · outside this range plants can't absorb nutrients
  • Best option: API pH test kit (~$10 at pet stores) · accurate enough for home hydro
  • DIY: a few drops of white vinegar or lemon juice per gallon lowers tap water pH
  • Upgrade option: Apera PH20 or BlueLab pH pen ($35–50) for long-term use
  • Check reservoir pH weekly · nutrients and evaporation both shift it
Weekly
Reservoir & Cube Care
Water · descaling cadence · rockwool cube care
Reservoir Management
Keep the water clean, cool, and dark and most root problems never start
  • Check the level every 2 to 3 days · top up with plain water between weekly feeds, since plants drink more water than nutrients
  • Full drain, clean, and refill every 2 to 3 weeks to clear salt buildup and algae
  • Keep the water below 72°F · warm water holds less oxygen and invites root rot · move the tower out of direct sun or add an airstone if it runs warm
  • Keep the reservoir lid sealed · light leaking in is the number one cause of algae blooms
  • EC creeps up as water evaporates and salts concentrate · if it climbs, top up with plain water rather than more nutrients
  • Add a few ml of 3% H₂O₂ to fresh water on refill to keep it oxygenated and suppress root rot
Ongoing
Water Quality
What goes in the tank matters as much as the nutrients
  • Tap water is fine for most growers · if it is heavily chlorinated, let it sit out overnight so the chlorine off-gasses before you add nutrients
  • Hard water runs alkaline and leaves white crust · it still works, but expect to pH down more often
  • Filtered or RO water gives the most control but starts with almost no minerals · your nutrients supply what the plants need
  • Always adjust pH after the nutrients are mixed in, to 5.5 to 6.5
Reference
Rockwool Cube Management
Choosing, prepping, and knowing when to toss your cubes
  • Use 2-inch cubes (Grodan Hugo or Delta) for a snug fit in Gardyn pod sleeves
  • Always pH-prep new cubes before seeding · soak 30 to 60 min in pH 5.5 water (full soak is in the DIY section)
  • Drain but never wring · rockwool holds the right moisture on its own, and squeezing collapses the air pockets roots need
  • Replace the cube with every new plant · old rockwool holds salts and bacteria
  • Never reuse a cube from a plant that had root rot or mildew · discard it
  • To reuse a healthy cube, sterilize first: soak in 1 part 3% H₂O₂ to 5 parts water, rinse, then re-prep to pH 5.5
Per plant
Plant Ledger. Log each pod, date planted, and quantity. Dates for sprout, first harvest, die-off and replant are calculated automatically. Based on the Gardyn Plant Tracker by Taylor Brewer.
Start of Watering Not set. Unlock to choose the day you first added water
